coredata.dc:title: In-situ formation of electron acceptor to inhibit charge separation of photo-electrochemical sensor of dopamine-based CdS/Au/GQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Ibrahim, Izwaharyanie
Lim, Hong Ngee
Huang, Nay Ming
coredata.dc:description: A versatile photo-electrochemical (PEC) sensor protocol was established to quantitatively monitor dopamine (DA) levels by utilizing a triple interconnected structure of cadmium sulfide (CdS) modified with gold and graphene quantum dots (Au/GQDs). The introduction of Au and GQDs on the photocatalytic active center of CdS act as a charge separation mediator and photosensitizer, respectively, which are favorable for charge separation and transportation and PEC conversion. When the CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode was utilized for DA sensing in a weak alkaline solution, DA was oxidized and converted to poly(dopamine) (PDA), which possesses abundant benzoquinone (BQ) groups that act as electron acceptors. Consequently, the electron acceptors formed in-situ on the surface of the photoelectrode, reducing the anodic photocurrent signal. Under the optimal conditions, the photocurrent decreased when the DA concentration increased in a dynamic working range from 0.1 to 350 µM and with a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.0078 μM. Herein, the proposed strategy involving photoelectron transfer between the electron acceptor and semiconductor provides a new and versatile protocol for PEC sensor development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo-electrochemical (PEC) sensor
Dopamine
CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode
Electron acceptor
Poly(dopamine)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of (A) the principle of visible light triggered PEC process and (B) charge transfer mechanism in (i) the absence and (ii) presence of DA based on CdS/Au/GQDs composite.
(A) LSV and (B) VOC curves of CdS, Au/GQDs, and CdS/Au/GQDs recorded under ‘on-off’ illumination.
(A) Photocurrent responses, (B) PL spectra, (C) EIS Nyquist plots, and (B) Bode-phase plots of CdS, Au/GQDs, and CdS/Au/GQDs in the absence and presence of 50 µM DA.

(A) Photocurrent response and (B) calibration curve with different DA concentration based on CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode. (C) Photocurrent response stability of CdS/Au/GQDs in the presence of 50 µM DA. (D) ΔI of CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode in the presence of various interfering substances. Error bars show the standard deviation of repeated measurements (n = 3).
Comparison of various PEC sensors with DA.
Results of DA detection in real urine samples using the proposed method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: In-situ formation of electron acceptor to inhibit charge separation of photo-electrochemical sensor of dopamine-based CdS/Au/GQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, UPM Serdang, Selangor 43400, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, UPM Serdang, Selangor 43400, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University Malaysia, Selangor Darul Ehsan 43900, Malaysia & College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Chemical Engineering
Xiamen University Malaysia, Selangor Darul Ehsan 43900
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Malaysia & College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Energy and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University Malaysia, Selangor Darul Ehsan 43900, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A versatile photo-electrochemical (PEC) sensor protocol was established to quantitatively monitor dopamine (DA) levels by utilizing a triple interconnected structure of cadmium sulfide (CdS) modified with gold and graphene quantum dots (Au/GQDs). The introduction of Au and GQDs on the photocatalytic active center of CdS act as a charge separation mediator and photosensitizer, respectively, which are favorable for charge separation and transportation and PEC conversion. When the CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode was utilized for DA sensing in a weak alkaline solution, DA was oxidized and converted to poly(dopamine) (PDA), which possesses abundant benzoquinone (BQ) groups that act as electron acceptors. Consequently, the electron acceptors formed in-situ on the surface of the photoelectrode, reducing the anodic photocurrent signal. Under the optimal conditions, the photocurrent decreased when the DA concentration increased in a dynamic working range from 0.1 to 350 µM and with a limit of detection (LoD) of 0.0078 μM. Herein, the proposed strategy involving photoelectron transfer between the electron acceptor and semiconductor provides a new and versatile protocol for PEC sensor development.

ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters function as messengers of neurological information across chemical synapses in the brain. These transmitters are composed of endogenous molecules and ensure communication between neighboring cells, leading to the control of our emotions and behaviors [1]. Among the diverse neurotransmitters, 3,4-dihydroxyphenethylamine, or dopamine (DA), belongs to the catecholamine family and has received continuous attention due to its significant roles in retaining physiological function as well as diseases pathogenesis [2]. Moreover, DA has been identified as a clinical biomarker for various neurological disorder linked to diseases, such as schizophrenia, cardiovascular, Alzheimer's, and Parkinson's diseases [3]. Despite the numerous individuals who currently and will suffer from such diseases, especially the elderly, an effective approach to detect the diseases still remains a clinical challenge. Therefore, a DA sensor with excellent sensitivity and selectivity is highly critical in both fundamental and applied science.

PEC sensors offer an inexpensive, highly sensitive, and rapid electroanalytical analysis technique with other advantages of low background signal, easy operation, and miniaturization. Recently, various PEC sensors for DA detection have been reported, which function according to the traditional hole oxidation mechanism [4,5]. In these sensors, DA acts as an effective electron donor for scavenging the photo-generated holes on the valance band (VB) of semiconductors and then inhibits the recombination of charge carriers [4,5], thus enhancing the photocurrent density. However, little attention has been given to the construction of PEC sensors for DA based on the interaction between the electron acceptor in the electrolyte and electron from the photo-excited semiconductor. Wang et al. [6] is the first group who developed the strategy for DA sensors based on the decreased anodic photocurrent. This work was followed by Hao et al. [7] who discovered that the anodic photocurrent of CdS decreases upon addition of DA in a weak alkaline electrolyte due to the formation of PDA, which is produced from oxidation of DA. It is known that PDA contains a wealth of benzoquinone (BQ) groups that act as electron acceptors for photo-excited CdS and, thus, could hinder electron transfer from CdS to the current collector, hence reducing the photocurrent. However, the utilization of CdS as a photoelectrode material suffers from a high recombination rate of photo-generated charge carriers and photocorrosion due to the strong oxidation capacity of photo-generated holes under light irradiation [7]. Therefore, the modification of CdS as a photoactive species is necessary to further enhance the stability and efficiency of the separation of charge carriers.

Previously, our group reported the modification of CdS using noble metal and quantum dot (QD)-based materials for PEC sensing application [8, 9]. Noble metal Au has been proven to serve as an electron conduction mediator for the efficient transportation of photo-generated charge carriers, as well as improving the photocatalytic activity of CdS [10]. Similar to CdS, GQDs display the ability to generate photo-excited charge carriers and facilitate the charge transport kinetics at the semiconductor-metal interface, which largely reduces the recombination rate [8]. Herein, the composite CdS/Au/GQDs material demonstrated a remarkable increase in photocurrent of ~2 times higher than that of bare CdS (581.09 mA cm−2).

Owing to the invincible synergic interaction between CdS and Au/GQDs and the notable advantages in PEC performance of this triply interconnected structure, CdS/Au/GQDs offers an effective photoactive material in PEC sensors for DA detection. Characterization of the composite material using FESEM, EDX, TEM, XRD, XPS, UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra, and PL is reported in our previous work, thus is not discussed in this study [8]. This work proposes a practical mechanism, whereby the in-situ formation of an electron acceptor occurs on the surface of photoactive species in a weak alkaline solution, based on meticulous characterization and analysis. As has been stated, PDA (formed via oxidation of DA) contains abundant BQ groups, which can efficiently decrease the photocurrent signal. Since many enzymes, such as tyrosinase, catalase, laccase, and peroxidase, can catalyze the oxidation of phenolic compounds to generate BQ, the proposed sensing protocol can be extended to the development of diverse biosensors. Therefore, the presented method based on interaction between the semiconductor and electron accepter may open a new avenue for the development of versatile PEC sensors.

CdS was synthesized according to the method reported by Yang et al. [12] with some modifications. In a typical experiment, the solvothermal process for the synthesis of CdS was carried out by mixing 0.457 g (6 mmol) CH4N2S powder in 20 mL n-butylamine. Next, 0.533 g (2 mmol) of Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O was completely dissolved in the mixture. With vigorous stirring, the final mixture was placed in a Teflon autoclave, sealed tightly, and subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 100°C for 24 h. The attained precipitate was centrifuged, thoroughly washed with ethanol and deionized water, then dried at room temperature for 24 h for further analysis. The final yellowish powder was denoted as CdS.

Au was obtained by the citrate reduction of HAuCl4 [13]. Briefly, 5 mg of GQDs was dissolved in 50 mL of a 1-mM HAuCl4 solution and then stirred vigorously for 30 min. The mixture was heated until boiling under stirring, followed by the addition of 5 mL of 40 mM trisodium citrate. The solution color changed from pale yellow to black and finally to a red wine color over several minutes. The reflux reaction then occurred for 60 min. After the solution was cooled down, centrifugation was performed to remove the extra free GQDs and trisodium citrate, then was re-dispersed in distilled water. For comparison, bare Au was also prepared in a similar way.

CdS/Au/GQDs was prepared using the reflux approach by first mixing 100 mL of the freshly prepared CdS powder at 10% w/v with the Au/GQDs solution. The mixture was left for 4 h to allow the Au/GQDs to grow on CdS. Afterward, the solution was placed in a three-necked flask and directly heated at 100°C in a reflux system under a nitrogen gas atmosphere for 1 h, which promoted the growth of Au/GQDs on the CdS structures. A final dark yellow colloidal solution was obtained. After cooling down, the resultant mixture was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and deionized water three times. Lastly, the precipitates were dried at room temperature for 24 h to yield dark yellowish powders of CdS/Au/GQDs.

The CdS/Au/GQDs-modified ITO sensor electrode was prepared using a simple doctor blade method. First, an ITO glass substrate (2 cm × 2 cm) was cleaned ultrasonically using acetone, ethanol, and deionized water. Then, 0.5 mL of the 50% w/v CdS/Au/GQDs powder was mixed with a nafion solution (0.1% w/v) and ground for several minutes to prepare a uniform CdS/Au/GQDs paste. Then, the CdS/Au/GQDs paste was cast onto the ITO glass by a doctor blade with tape as the spacer. The resultant CdS/Au/GQDs thin film was dried at 70°C and calcined at 150°C for 1 h in an inert atmosphere. For comparison, CdS- and Au/GQDs/ITO-modified electrodes were also fabricated by following the same procedure.

PEC sensing was performed using a three-electrode system that included the active material as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. PEC tests were carried out at a constant potential of 0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and using 0.1 M PBS (pH 9) containing 0.5 M TEA (as the electron donor) as the electrolyte. DA was detected photoelectrochemically by introducing different concentrations of DA (0.1-350 µM) into the electrolyte solution. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the electrolyte containing 0.1 M KCl and 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) was used, and the frequency range was set from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz at open circuit potential. A 150-W low-cost solar simulator (Abet Technologies) was used as the light source in this experiment. Solar simulation was performed by setting a distance of 10 cm between the photoactive site and light source to achieve an illumination field with a diameter of 35 mm and uniformity of +/- 25%. Before the PEC test, the samples were carefully washed with 0.1 M PBS (pH 9) and immersed in the electrolyte for 10 min to fully remove the absorption molecules. Immediately after the background photocurrent was stabilized, the photocurrent response was recorded as the excitation light was turned on and off. The photocurrent signal was measured and analyzed on a computer-controlled Versa-STAT-3 electrochemical analyzer from Princeton Applied Research.

The electron lifetime (τe) was calculated according to Eq. (1), which employs the maximum frequency peaks (ƒmax) in the Bode phase plots:
(1)
τ e
= 1 /
(
2 π
f
m a x
)

A schematic illustration of the principle of the visible light triggered PEC process based on the CdS/Au/GQDs composite is illustrated in Fig. 1 A. Under visible light illumination, CdS/Au/GQDs was favorable to transport the photo-generated electrons to the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode, which subsequently produced a photocurrent. This effective transport is attributable to the effective matching of the conduction bands (CBs) of CdS and Au/GQDs. Moreover, the proposed mechanism for PEC sensing of DA is displayed in detail in Fig. 1B, which clearly differs from the conventional approach that involves the hole (h+) oxidation mechanism. The charge transfer process of CdS/Au/GQDs upon light illumination can be separated into five steps: (1) excitation of electrons from the valance band (VB) to CB of GQDs, leaving holes in the VB; (2) electron injection from the CB of GQDs into Au; (3) plasmonic Au induced hot electrons injected into CdS; (4) CdS simultaneously excited electrons to the CB and generated holes in the VB; and (5) electrons were driven to the ITO electrode, where the generation of an anodic photocurrent is due to the lower energy level of the CB of ITO [8]. Meanwhile, the holes in the VB of GQDs and CdS were transferred to the electrolyte containing triethanolamine (TEA) as the electron donor (Fig. 1B(i)). Subsequently, the reduction in electron-hole (e−-h+) recombination occurred, which amplified the anodic photocurrent signal.

Moreover, the addition of DA in the electrolyte composed of weak alkaline solution (PBS, pH = 9) generated the in-situ formation of PDA with numerous BQ groups that act as electron acceptors in the PEC system [6]. The presence of electron acceptor inhibited the charge separation and subsequently affected the produced anodic photocurrent. Moreover, the inhibition by BQ can be related to the formation of a recombination complex, where the photo-induced electrons at the CB of CdS and GQDs were transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of BQ (Fig. 1B(ii)). Then, the electron transfer of CdS to the ITO electrode by BQ significantly reduced the anodic photocurrent and enabled the indirect detection of DA.

The PEC activities of the CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode were evaluated using linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) under chopped light illumination at applied potentials of -1.0 to 0.9 V vs. Ag/AgCl with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The LSV measurements were recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH9) containing 0.5 M TEA solution. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, CdS/Au/GQDs exhibited a substantially enhanced photocurrent of 0.63 mA cm−2 about 4 and 12 times greater than those of CdS (0.16 mA cm−2) and Au/GQDs (0.05 mA cm−2), respectively, under light illumination. This can be attributed to the fact that Au aids the separation of charge carriers to the current collector and, thus, improves the charge carriers’ lifetime by the incorporation of GQDs, which subsequently reduces the electrochemical impedance of the nanocomposite [8,14].

A study of the open-circuit voltage (VOC) under ‘on-off’ light illumination was conducted to consider the interfacial kinetics between the photoelectrode and electrolyte and the lifetime of the charge carrier. As portrayed in Fig. 2B, a negative shift in VOC is clearly seen under light illumination. The photovoltage of CdS shifted from -0.11 V to a more negative potential after the incorporation of Au and GQDs. Specifically, CdS/Au/GQDs showed an ~8.5 fold higher photovoltage (-0.89 V) than the blank CdS, which can be attributed to the cooperative synergy of CdS with Au/GQDs to induce a better charge carrier separation and electron accumulation [15]. Nevertheless, the Au/GQDs (0.46V) presented a lower VOC response due to its poor light absorption ability.

The photocurrent responses of the modified photoelectrodes under ‘on-off’ illumination were explored to further corroborate the observations in LSV and VOC measurements. The amperometric curves were recorded at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl in PBS (pH 9) and with a TEA electrolyte. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the current-time curves of all photoelectrodes display a steep increase to a saturated value when the light was ‘on’ then decayed rapidly to its original value upon light ‘off.’ However, CdS/Au/GQDs apparently exhibited an invincible photocurrent performance of 0.52 mA cm−2, which is comparably higher than those of CdS (0.22 mA cm−2) and Au/GQDs (0.16 mA cm−2)-fabricated photoelectrodes. These results provide explicit evidence that the multi-functional hybrid structure of CdS/Au/GQDs is responsible for its remarkable performance in PEC activities, which is also closely related to the presence of TEA as an electron donor. Based on our previous study, TEA as an electron donor can scavenge empty holes in the semiconductor, thus producing a stable and intense photocurrent signal as compared to the absence of TEA [9,16]. This favorable outcome is due to the reduction of e−-h+ recombination in the semiconductor material. Upon introduction of 50 µM DA, the photocurrent signal decreased at one-order of magnitude than CdS/Au/GQDs. It was found that BQ (formed by oxidation of DA to PDA) acts as an electron acceptor in the weak alkaline electrolyte and inhibits the electron transfer to the ITO electrode, as shown in Fig. 1B.

The photo-induced electron transfer between the photoactive materials, CdS/Au/GQDs and BQ, was confirmed by photoluminescence (PL) spectra, as shown in Fig. 3B. In the PL spectra, CdS displays the strongest PL band, and the amplified emission intensity of CdS at ~529 nm is attributed to the faster e−-h+ recombination rate. Besides, a much weaker profile with about 2-fold decrease in intensity was detected for CdS/Au/GQDs (~519 nm) due to the effective segregation of photo-generated charge carriers. The PL curve for CdS/Au/GQDs in the presence of DA shows an overwhelming quenching effect. The PL quenching of CdS/Au/GQDS/DA is presumably attributed to electron transfer caused by the in-situ formation of BQ as electron acceptor on the photoelectrode surface, which quenches the fluorescence of CdS/Au/GQDs [17]. This result suggests that DA-BQ effectively quenches CdS/Au/GQDs, providing an efficient electron transfer process from the photoelectrode to oxidize DA.

To better understand the effect of DA on the CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode surface, the interface behavior was investigated under light illumination. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were obtained in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1). Fig. 3C displays the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of each photoelectrode, before and after the addition of DA. The semicircular Nyquist plots of CdS/Au/GQDs show the smallest semicircle, which is ascribed to the effective separation of photo-generated charge carriers and a faster interfacial charge transfer process. However, a larger semicircle, about 0.7 times larger than that of CdS/Au/GQDs, was observed after DA was incorporated on the surface of CdS/Au/GQDs. This phenomenon is attributed to the presence of an electron acceptor that inhibited the transfer of electrons to the substrate and, hence, resulted in a shorter electron lifetime. Herein, a bode-phase plot was obtained to explain in detail the relationship between the semicircle in the Nyquist plot and electron lifetime (τe). Based on Fig. 3D, the peak frequencies of CdS/Au/GQDs and CdS/Au/GQDs/DA were found to be 158.49 and 251.19 Hz, respectively, with calculated τe values of 1.0 and 0.63 ms. The smaller τe of CdS/Au/GQDs/DA is due to presence of electron acceptors, which conclusively justifies the results of the EIS measurement.

To explore the stability of the photoelectrode, the time-dependent photocurrent under ‘on-off’ illumination every 30 s was measured in PBS (pH 9) and TEA solution containing 50 µM DA (Fig. 4C). The photocurrent response showed no obvious decline after 25 cycles (1530 s). Moreover, the long-term storage stability of the sensor was also investigated. CdS/Au/GQDs was stored at 4°C in a refrigerator, and the electrode with 50 µM DA was evaluated in intervals of 1 day. The photocurrent response remained more than 90% of the initial response after two weeks (Fig. S1), which demonstrates the electrode's excellent long-term storage stability. Besides, the reproducibility of CdS/Au/GQDs was also investigated by using five independent photoelectrodes towards 50 µM DA. The obtained relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 2.54% (Fig. S2) reveals good reproducibility of the photoelectrode.

The selectivity of the photoelectrode was evaluated by recording the photocurrent responses of CdS/Au/GQDs in electrolyte containing 90 µM of various interfering substances, including ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), and glucose (Glu), and 30 µM DA (Fig. 4D). The results reveal that DA exhibited the highest ΔI (initial response in absence of interfering substance – response after addition of interfering substance), even with a concentration 3 times lower that the other interfering substances, which indicates the highly selectivity of CdS/Au/GQDs towards DA. Additionally, the accuracy and feasibility of the strategy were evaluated on a real sample of human urine (Table 2 ). Three different urine samples with a dilution of 100 times were found adequate to acquire a quantitative recovery of 98.6-102.4 % upon spiking with 10 µM of DA, which was satisfactory and promising in the direct detection of DA. Ultimately, this detection mechanism could be potentially applied to the determination of DA in real samples.

A novel PEC sensing protocol was constructed based on photo-induced electron transfer between the photoactive CdS/Au/GQDs material and electron acceptor. Undoubtedly, this triply interconnected structure exhibits an efficient interfacial charge transfer, which enhances the photogenerated electron lifetime. Ultimately, the amplified PEC activity suggests that the composite material is beneficial for PEC sensing of DA. In addition, upon employing this photoelectrode in a PEC sensor for DA detection, the sensor showed high sensitivity, selectivity, stability, reproducibility, fast response, wide linear range (0.1-350 μM), and relatively low detection limit of 0.0078 μM. Conclusively, the CdS/Au/GQDs photoelectrode provides a promising platform for the future research and development of high-performance PEC sensors for DA detection.
Izwaharyanie Ibrahim: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Hong Ngee Lim: Supervision, Conceptualization. Nay Ming Huang: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by GP-IPS (9537500) from Universiti Putra Malaysia.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2020.137013.
